content:
# Genesis Chapter 22 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folio 12r–12v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_022_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_022_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 17–18 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0021

---

*The Aqedah — The Testing of Abraham (vv. 1–19)*

**22:1** And it came about [וַיְהִי] after these things [אַחַר הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה] that God [הָאֱלֹהִים] tested [נִסָּה — nissah] Abraham [אֶת-אַבְרָהָם] and said to him [וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו]: Abraham! [אַבְרָהָם] And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Here I am [הִנֵּנִי].

**22:2** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Take [קַח-נָא] — please — your son [אֶת-בִּנְךָ] — your only son [אֶת-יְחִידְךָ] — whom you love [אֲשֶׁר-אָהַבְתָּ] — Isaac [אֶת-יִצְחָק] — and go [וְלֶךְ-לְךָ] to you [לְךָ] to the land of Moriah [אֶל-אֶרֶץ הַמֹּרִיָּה — ha-Moriyyah] and offer him up there [וְהַעֲלֵהוּ שָׁם] as a burnt offering [לְעֹלָה] upon one of the mountains [עַל אַחַד הֶהָרִים] that I will say to you [אֲשֶׁר אֹמַר אֵלֶיךָ].

**22:3** And Abraham rose early in the morning [וַיַּשְׁכֵּם אַבְרָהָם בַּבֹּקֶר] and saddled his donkey [וַיַּחֲבֹשׁ אֶת-חֲמֹרוֹ] and took two of his young men [וַיִּקַּח אֶת-שְׁנֵי נְעָרָיו] with him [אִתּוֹ] and Isaac his son [וְאֵת יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ] and he split [וַיְבַקַּע] the wood [עֲצֵי] of the burnt offering [עֹלָה] and arose [וַיָּקָם] and went [וַיֵּלֶךְ] to the place [אֶל-הַמָּקוֹם] that God had said to him [אֲשֶׁר-אָמַר-לוֹ הָאֱלֹהִים].

**22:4** On the third day [בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי] Abraham lifted up his eyes [וַיִּשָּׂא אַבְרָהָם אֶת-עֵינָיו] and saw [וַיַּרְא] the place [אֶת-הַמָּקוֹם] from afar [מֵרָחֹק].

**22:5** And Abraham said [וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָהָם] to his young men [אֶל-נְעָרָיו]: Stay here [שְׁבוּ-לָכֶם פֹּה] with the donkey [עִם-הַחֲמוֹר] and I [וַאֲנִי] and the boy [וְהַנַּעַר] will go [נֵלְכָה] thus far [עַד-כֹּה] and worship [וְנִשְׁתַּחֲוֶה] and return [וְנָשׁוּבָה] to you [אֲלֵיכֶם].

**22:6** And Abraham took [וַיִּקַּח אַבְרָהָם] the wood [אֶת-עֲצֵי] of the burnt offering [הָעֹלָה] and placed [it] [וַיָּשֶׂם] upon Isaac his son [עַל-יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ] and he took in his hand [וַיִּקַּח בְּיָדוֹ] the fire [אֶת-הָאֵשׁ] and the knife [וְאֶת-הַמַּאֲכֶלֶת] and the two of them [וַיֵּלְכוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם] walked together [יַחְדָּו].

**22:7** And Isaac said [וַיֹּאמֶר יִצְחָק] to Abraham his father [אֶל-אַבְרָהָם אָבִיו] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: My father! [אָבִי] And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Here I am [הִנֶּנִּי], my son [בְּנִי]. And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Look — the fire [הִנֵּה הָאֵשׁ] and the wood [וְהָעֵצִים] — but where is [וְאַיֵּה] the lamb [הַשֶּׂה] for the burnt offering [לְעֹלָה]?

**22:8** And Abraham said [וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָהָם]: God will provide for himself [יִרְאֶה-לוֹ אֱלֹהִים — yir'eh-lo] the lamb [הַשֶּׂה] for the burnt offering [לְעֹלָה] — my son [בְּנִי]. And the two of them [וַיֵּלְכוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם] walked together [יַחְדָּו].

**22:9** And they came [וַיָּבֹאוּ] to the place [אֶל-הַמָּקוֹם] that God had said to him [אֲשֶׁר אָמַר-לוֹ הָאֱלֹהִים] and Abraham built [וַיִּבֶן שָׁם אַבְרָהָם] there the altar [אֶת-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ] and arranged [וַיַּעֲרֹךְ] the wood [אֶת-הָעֵצִים] and bound [וַיַּעֲקֹד] Isaac his son [אֶת-יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ] and placed him [וַיָּשֶׂם אֹתוֹ] on the altar [עַל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ] — upon the wood [מִמַּעַל לָעֵצִים].

**22:10** And Abraham stretched out his hand [וַיִּשְׁלַח אַבְרָהָם אֶת-יָדוֹ] and took the knife [וַיִּקַּח אֶת-הַמַּאֲכֶלֶת] to slaughter [לִשְׁחֹט] his son [אֶת-בְּנוֹ].

**22:11** And the angel of the LORD [וּמַלְאַךְ יְהוָה] called out to him [קָרָא אֵלָיו] from the heavens [מִן-הַשָּׁמַיִם] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Abraham! Abraham! [אַבְרָהָם אַבְרָהָם] And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Here I am [הִנֵּנִי].

**22:12** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Do not stretch out your hand [אַל-תִּשְׁלַח יָדְךָ] to the boy [אֶל-הַנַּעַר] and do nothing [וְאַל-תַּעַשׂ לוֹ מְאוּמָה] to him — for now I know [כִּי עַתָּה יָדַעְתִּי] that you are one who fears God [כִּי-יְרֵא אֱלֹהִים אַתָּה] — and you did not withhold [וְלֹא חָשַׂכְתָּ] your son [אֶת-בִּנְךָ] — your only son [אֶת-יְחִידְךָ] — from me [מִמֶּנִּי].

**22:13** And Abraham lifted up his eyes [וַיִּשָּׂא אַבְרָהָם אֶת-עֵינָיו] and saw [וַיַּרְא] — and look [וְהִנֵּה] — a ram [אַיִל] behind [אַחַר] caught [נֶאֱחַז] in the thicket [בַּסְּבַךְ] by its horns [בְּקַרְנָיו] — and Abraham went [וַיֵּלֶךְ אַבְרָהָם] and took the ram [וַיִּקַּח אֶת-הָאַיִל] and offered it up [וַיַּעֲלֵהוּ] as a burnt offering [לְעֹלָה] in place of his son [תַּחַת בְּנוֹ].

**22:14** And Abraham called [וַיִּקְרָא אַבְרָהָם] the name of that place [שֵׁם-הַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא] YHWH-yireh [יְהוָה יִרְאֶה] — as it is said [אֲשֶׁר יֵאָמֵר] today [הַיּוֹם]: On the mount of the LORD [בְּהַר יְהוָה] it shall be seen [יֵרָאֶה].

---

*The Renewed Oath and Blessing (vv. 15–18)*

**22:15** And the angel of the LORD [וַיִּקְרָא מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה] called out to Abraham [אֶל-אַבְרָהָם] a second time [שֵׁנִית] from the heavens [מִן-הַשָּׁמַיִם].

**22:16** And said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: By myself I have sworn [בִּי נִשְׁבַּעְתִּי] — declares the LORD [נְאֻם-יְהוָה] — for [כִּי] because you have done this thing [אֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתָ אֶת-הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה] and have not withheld [וְלֹא חָשַׂכְתָּ] your son [אֶת-בִּנְךָ] — your only son [אֶת-יְחִידֶךָ] —

**22:17** that blessing I will bless you [כִּי-בָרֵךְ אֲבָרֶכְךָ] and multiplying I will multiply [וְהַרְבָּה אַרְבֶּה] your offspring [אֶת-זַרְעֲךָ] as the stars of the heavens [כְּכוֹכְבֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם] and as the sand [וְכַחוֹל] that is on the shore of the sea [אֲשֶׁר עַל-שְׂפַת הַיָּם] — and your offspring [וְיִרַשׁ זַרְעֲךָ] shall possess [יִירַשׁ] the gate of his enemies [אֵת שַׁעַר אֹיְבָיו].

**22:18** And in your offspring [וְהִתְבָּרֲכוּ בְזַרְעֲךָ] all the nations of the earth shall be blessed [כֹּל גּוֹיֵי הָאָרֶץ — hitbarekhu] — because [עֵקֶב] you have listened to [אֲשֶׁר שָׁמַעְתָּ] my voice [בְּקֹלִי].

**22:19** And Abraham returned [וַיָּשָׁב אַבְרָהָם] to his young men [אֶל-נְעָרָיו] and they arose [וַיָּקֻמוּ] and walked together [וַיֵּלְכוּ יַחְדָּו] to Beer-sheba [אֶל-בְּאֵר שָׁבַע] and Abraham dwelt [וַיֵּשֶׁב אַבְרָהָם] at Beer-sheba [בִּבְאֵר שָׁבַע].

---

*The Genealogy of Nahor — Rebekah Introduced (vv. 20–24)*

**22:20** And it came about [וַיְהִי] after these things [אַחֲרֵי הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה] that it was told [וַיֻּגַּד] to Abraham [לְאַבְרָהָם] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Look — Milcah [הִנֵּה יָלְדָה מִלְכָּה] has also borne [גַּם-הִוא] children [יְלָדִים] to Nahor [לְנָחוֹר] your brother [אָחִיךָ].

**22:21** Uz his firstborn [אֶת-עוּץ בְּכֹרוֹ] and Buz his brother [וְאֶת-בּוּז אָחִיו] and Kemuel [וְאֶת-קְמוּאֵל] the father of Aram [אֲבִי אֲרָם].

**22:22** And Kesed [וְאֶת-כֶּשֶׂד] and Hazo [וְאֶת-חֲזוֹ] and Pildash [וְאֶת-פִּלְדָּשׁ] and Jidlaph [וְאֶת-יִדְלָף] and Bethuel [וְאֶת-בְּתוּאֵל].

**22:23** And Bethuel fathered [וּבְתוּאֵל יָלַד] Rebekah [אֶת-רִבְקָה] — these eight [אֶת-שְׁמֹנָה אֵלֶּה] Milcah bore [יָלְדָה מִלְכָּה] to Nahor [לְנָחוֹר] the brother of Abraham [אֲחִי אַבְרָהָם].

**22:24** And his concubine [וּפִילַגְשׁוֹ] — and her name [וּשְׁמָהּ] was Reumah [רְאוּמָה] — she also bore [וַתֵּלֶד גַּם-הִוא] Tebah [אֶת-טֶבַח] and Gaham [וְאֶת-גַּחַם] and Tahash [וְאֶת-תַּחַשׁ] and Maacah [וְאֶת-מַעֲכָה].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**On the structure of the Aqedah — reading from Leningrad Codex folio 12r–12v:**
The Leningrad Codex presents Genesis 22 beginning mid-column on folio 12r (the right column, clearly marked with the parasha section marker *pe* for the open section beginning at 22:1). The three-column layout of folio 12r is readable; the chapter proceeds through approximately v. 14 on this page, then continues on folio 12v which picks up mid-chapter with the second divine speech (vv. 15ff.) and the Nahor genealogy closing at v. 24. The second manuscript image is photographed inverted but the Hebrew text is clearly legible once oriented. The Codex Vaticanus pages 17–18 carry the Greek LXX text of this chapter: page 17 (online reference 0021) contains the Aqedah narrative, and page 18 (online reference 0022) carries the latter portion including the genealogy and continues into Genesis 23. A chapter division marker *CAP. XXV* is visible at the top of Vaticanus page 18, and a marginal *CAP. IIII* notation appears at the right margin — these reflect the ancient Greek chapter enumeration rather than the modern chapter system.

**22:1 — "tested" (*nissah*, נִסָּה):**
The verb *nissah* (נִסָּה, Piel of נ-ס-ה) means to test, to prove, to put to the proof. It does not mean "to tempt" in the sense of enticement to evil — this is the Hebrew word for the proving of a person's character under pressure. The same root forms the noun *massah* (מַסָּה, "testing/trial") used in Exod 17:7 for the site where Israel tested God. Here God is the subject; Abraham is the object. The LXX Vaticanus reads *ho theos epeirazen ton Abraam* (ὁ θεὸς ἐπείραζεν τὸν Αβρααμ) — using *peirazō* (πειράζω), which can mean both "test" and "tempt." James 1:13 insists God "tempts no one," implicitly distinguishing the sense. I transliterate *nissah* and gloss it above: the narrator names what God is doing before the reader sees a word of dialogue, removing all false suspense about divine intention. The test is real; the divine purpose is provable, not malicious.

**22:2 — Fourfold identification of Isaac:**
The divine command builds with devastating deliberateness: "your son [בִּנְךָ] — your only son [יְחִידְךָ] — whom you love [אֲשֶׁר-אָהַבְתָּ] — Isaac [יִצְחָק]." Each term intensifies the demand. *Yāchîd* (יָחִיד, "only/unique/solitary") is the key word — Isaac is the only son of promise, the one through whom the covenant continues. The word "love" (*'āhab*, אָהַב) appears here for the first time in the Bible — the first recorded use of "love" in scripture is a father's love for his son, commanded to be surrendered. The LXX Vaticanus renders *yāchîd* as *agapētos* (ἀγαπητός), "beloved" — an interpretive shift that reads the unique-sonship through the lens of love. Paul's language of Christ as God's "beloved Son" (*huios agapētos*, cf. Rom 8:32, echoing Gen 22:16) draws on this LXX rendering. I retain the Hebrew force: "your only son," not "your beloved son," preserving the precise legal and covenantal claim.

**22:2 — "the land of Moriah" (*'erets ha-Moriyyah*, אֶרֶץ הַמֹּרִיָּה):**
The name *ha-Moriyyah* (הַמֹּרִיָּה) is transliterated and preserved. Its etymology is debated: (a) from *ra'ah* (רָאָה, "to see") — "the land of vision/seeing," which would connect to the naming of v. 14 (*YHWH-yireh*); (b) from *yārē'* (יָרֵא, "to fear") — "the land of the fear [of God]," connecting to v. 12 (*yĕrē' 'ĕlōhîm*, "one who fears God"); (c) from *yārāh* (יָרָה, "to teach/instruct") — "the land of teaching," connecting to the later Jerusalem temple as the place of Torah. The 2 Chronicles 3:1 connection is explicit: "Solomon began to build the house of the LORD in Jerusalem on Mount Moriah." The word resists clean etymology — it may be an ancient place name into which multiple meanings were read. I transliterate it (*ha-Moriyyah*) and annotate rather than attempt a translation that privileges one etymology.

**22:3 — "saddled his donkey... split the wood" — the wordless obedience:**
The rapidity of the verbal sequence in v. 3 is the most theologically striking feature of Abraham's response: *wayyaškem... wayyachăbōš... wayyiqqach... wayyĕbaqqĕa'... wayyāqom... wayyēlek* — "and he rose early... and he saddled... and he took... and he split... and he arose... and he went." Six verbs of action with no speech, no delay, no hesitation, no recorded interior response. The laconic narration is itself a theological statement. I have preserved every verb. The LXX preserves this sequence faithfully with a series of *kai* + aorist verbs, confirming the rhythm of the Hebrew.

**22:5 — "I and the boy will go... and worship and return" — both verbs first-person plural:**
The verbs in v. 5 are conspicuous: *nēlĕkāh* ("we will go"), *wĕništachăweh* ("and we will worship"), *wĕnāšûbāh* ("and we will return") — all first-person plural inclusive, covering both Abraham and Isaac. Abraham says *both* of them will return. Whether Abraham is (a) deceiving the servants about his intention; (b) trusting that God will provide a substitute; or (c) trusting that God can raise Isaac from the dead (so Heb 11:19) is a profound interpretive question to which the text gives no explicit answer. I have rendered the verbs literally, preserving the grammatical plurality that drives the question. The LXX Vaticanus renders the verbs identically with Greek first-person plural forms: *poreusometha... kai proskunēsantes* (*kai apostraphēsometha*).

**22:6 — "the knife" (*ha-ma'ăkelet*, הַמַּאֲכֶלֶת):**
The word *ma'ăkelet* (מַאֲכֶלֶת) is derived from *'ākal* (אָכַל, "to eat/consume") — it is literally "the eating-thing," a large knife used for slaughter, a consumer of flesh. The root *'ākal* is the same used for fire "consuming" offerings. I render it "knife" in the translation body but note this etymological texture here. The LXX uses *machaira* (μάχαιρα, "sword, large knife"), a general word without the consuming connotation.

**22:8 — "God will provide for himself" (*yir'eh-lo*, יִרְאֶה-לוֹ):**
This phrase is the theological hinge of the chapter. The verb is *yir'eh* (יִרְאֶה, Qal imperfect of *rā'āh*, "to see") with the reflexive/dative *lo* (לוֹ, "for himself"). The construction *yir'eh-lo* means "he will see to it for himself" — "God will provide" in the conventional English rendering, though "provide" obscures the root. The direct rendering is: "God will see — for himself — the lamb." The verb *rā'āh* ("to see") then governs the entire naming theology of the chapter: God "sees" the lamb (v. 8), Abraham names the place *YHWH-yireh* ("the LORD will see/provide," v. 14), and the etiological proverb runs "on the mount of the LORD it shall be seen/provided" (v. 14, *yērā'eh*, Niphal passive: "he shall be seen"). The see-ing and the provide-ing are the same act. I transliterate *yir'eh-lo* in the translation text and annotate here.

**22:9 — "bound" (*wayyaʿăqōd*, וַיַּעֲקֹד):**
The verb *'āqad* (עָקַד) appears only here in the entire Hebrew Bible. It means to bind the legs (as one ties an animal for slaughter). The uniqueness of this verb — its hapax status — is remarkable: an entire chapter, indeed an entire tradition, named "the Aqedah" (הָעֲקֵדָה, "the Binding") from a word used only once in the Bible. The verb is preserved visually in the Leningrad Codex on folio 12r; the LXX Vaticanus renders it *edesen* (ἔδησεν), "bound/tied," from *deō* (δέω) — a more general word. I render it "bound" to reflect both the specificity and the force of the Hebrew; there is no English equivalent for *'āqad* as a technical term for binding an animal's legs before slaughter.

**22:11 — "Abraham! Abraham!" — the doubled name:**
The repetition of the name — *'abrāhām, 'abrāhām* — is unique among divine addresses in the Aqedah. Compare the single "Abraham!" of v. 1. The doubling in v. 11 signals urgency, intimacy, a stay of execution. The same doubling occurs at divine calls of critical importance: "Moses! Moses!" (Exod 3:4), "Samuel! Samuel!" (1 Sam 3:10), "Saul! Saul!" (Acts 9:4). The LXX Vaticanus reads *Abraam, Abraam* (Αβρααμ, Αβρααμ), faithfully preserving the doubling.

**22:12 — "now I know" (*kî 'attāh yādaʿtî*, כִּי עַתָּה יָדַעְתִּי):**
One of the most theologically vexed phrases in the chapter. The angel — speaking as the divine voice — says "now I know that you fear God." Does this imply that God did not know before? Does omniscience require testing to become knowledge? The commentaries from Rashi to Kierkegaard wrestle here. I render the Hebrew literally without harmonizing: "now I know." The word *'attāh* (עַתָּה, "now") is emphatic — the moment of the raising of the knife is the moment of the divine knowing. Whether this reflects a genuine epistemological moment for God or a narrative/rhetorical idiom for divine confirmation is not for the literal translator to adjudicate. The LXX Vaticanus reads *nun gar egnōn* (νῦν γὰρ ἔγνων), "for now I have known" — with the particle *gar* (for/because) connecting it to what follows. The Hebrew has no connective particle here; *kî* in *kî 'attāh* is the asseverative "indeed" or "for," modifying the preceding "do not lay your hand on the boy."

**22:13 — "a ram... behind" (*'ayil... 'achar*, אַיִל... אַחַר) — the Masoretic textual crux:**
The Masoretic text (as read in the Leningrad Codex) reads *'ayil 'achar nĕ'ĕchaz bassĕbak bĕqarnāyw* — "a ram — behind — caught in the thicket by its horns." The word *'achar* (אַחַר, "behind/after") is grammatically awkward here; it does not modify the ram (which would require agreement) but seems to indicate spatial placement: the ram was *behind* Abraham (he had to turn to see it, having just been looking at Isaac on the altar). Some manuscripts and the Samaritan Pentateuch read *'echād* (אֶחָד, "one") instead of *'achar* ("behind/after") — "a ram — one — caught in the thicket." The LXX Vaticanus reads *krion hena katechomenon en phutō sabek tois kerasin* (κριὸν ἕνα κατεχόμενον ἐν φυτῷ σαβεκ τοῖς κέρασιν) — reading *hena* (one) not *opisthen* (behind), aligning with the *'echad* reading. The Leningrad Codex clearly has *'achar*; I have rendered it "behind" in the translation text and note the *'echad* variant. The difference is not theologically decisive but the Masoretic form is the more difficult reading (*lectio difficilior*) and therefore generally preferred by text critics.

**22:14 — YHWH-yireh (*יְהוָה יִרְאֶה*) and the etiological proverb:**
Abraham names the place *YHWH-yireh* — "the LORD will see/provide." The conventional English rendering "the LORD will provide" captures the pragmatic sense but loses the visual root. The verb *rā'āh* means primarily "to see" — hence the name is simultaneously "the LORD sees" and "the LORD provides," because divine seeing and divine provision are the same act in this theology. The etiological proverb that follows — *bĕhar YHWH yērā'eh* (בְּהַר יְהוָה יֵרָאֶה) — is a Niphal passive: "on the mount of the LORD *it shall be seen*" — or, with reflexive Niphal force: "on the mount of the LORD *he shall appear/be seen*." The ambiguity is irreducible in Hebrew; the Niphal of *rā'āh* can mean either "to be seen" or "to appear" (i.e., God himself appearing). I render it "it shall be seen" while noting the possible reflexive reading. The LXX Vaticanus renders the name as *Kyrios eiden* (Κύριος εἶδεν, "the Lord saw") and the proverb as *en tō orei Kyrios ōphthē* (ἐν τῷ ὄρει Κύριος ὤφθη, "on the mountain the Lord was seen/appeared") — using *horaō* in its passive *ōphthē* form, confirming the ambiguity.

**22:15–16 — "By myself I have sworn" (*bî nišbaʿtî*):**
The formula *bî nišbaʿtî* is a divine self-oath — God swears by himself (*bî*, "by me"). This construction appears because there is no greater by which God can swear (cf. Heb 6:13). It is theologically unprecedented in the Abraham cycle; the earlier promise at 12:1-3 and 15:1-21 was a covenant-with-sign, not a formal oath by divine self-affirmation. The Aqedah produces the sworn, unconditional, irrevocable form of the Abrahamic promise. The formula *nĕ'um-YHWH* (נְאֻם-יְהוָה, "declares the LORD," "oracle of the LORD") accompanies only this self-oath in Genesis — further elevating the gravity of vv. 16-18 above all earlier divine speech to Abraham.

**22:17 — "possess the gate of his enemies" (*yîraš zar'ăkā 'ēt šaʿar 'ōyĕbāyw*):**
The phrase "possess/inherit the gate of his enemies" is a military and civic idiom: to possess the gate is to control the city, since the gate was the seat of legal, commercial, and military power. The singular *šaʿar* (gate) with plural "enemies" is preserved — not "gates" but "gate" as a collective singular for all gates of all enemies. I render it word for word. The LXX renders it *klēronomēsei ho sperma sou tas poleis tōn hupanatiōn* (κληρονομήσει ὁ σπέρμα σου τὰς πόλεις τῶν ἐναντίων) — "your offspring shall inherit the cities of the adversaries" — broadening "gate" to "cities." I follow the Hebrew's more concrete and specific form.

**22:18 — *hitbarekhu* (הִתְבָּרֲכוּ) — Hithpael reflexive or passive:**
The verb *hitbarekhu* (הִתְבָּרֲכוּ) is the Hithpael third-person plural of *bārak* (בָּרַךְ, "to bless"). The Hithpael is the reflexive stem in Hebrew, suggesting: "all the nations of the earth shall bless themselves [in/by your offspring]" — meaning they will invoke your offspring's name in blessing formulas, saying "may I be blessed as Abraham's offspring." But the Hithpael can also function passively: "shall be blessed" (so Paul reads at Gal 3:8, citing the LXX; cf. Acts 3:25). The parallel passage at 12:3 uses the Niphal (*wĕnibrĕkû*, וְנִבְרְכוּ), unambiguously passive: "shall be blessed." The Hithpael here — unique to 22:18 and 26:4 among the five Abrahamic promise passages — opens the reflexive reading. I transliterate *hitbarekhu* in the translation, render it "shall be blessed," and annotate the Hithpael ambiguity here. The LXX Vaticanus reads *eneulogēthēsontai en tō spermati sou panta ta ethnē tēs gēs* (ἐνευλογηθήσονται ἐν τῷ σπέρματί σου πάντα τὰ ἔθνη τῆς γῆς) — a passive future (*eneulogēthēsontai*), removing the reflexive possibility and reading unambiguously as "shall be blessed." Paul's theological argument in Galatians 3 depends on this passive reading.

**22:19 — "Abraham returned to his young men" — Isaac not mentioned:**
The most haunting textual silence in the chapter. After the Aqedah, the narrative says "Abraham returned to his young men and they arose and walked together to Beer-sheba and Abraham dwelt at Beer-sheba." Isaac is not named. He does not appear as a subject of any verb. The narrative that began with "they walked together" (v. 6, v. 8) ends with "they walked together" (v. 19) — but the pronoun refers to Abraham and his servants, not Abraham and Isaac. Whether Isaac was present (the usual assumption) or separated at this point is a genuine textual gap. Midrashic tradition and some ancient interpreters filled this gap dramatically (e.g., traditions of Isaac going to study at the school of Shem and Eber). I render the Hebrew as it stands without resolution, preserving the conspicuous absence.

**22:20–24 — The genealogy of Nahor — Rebekah introduced:**
The genealogy closes the chapter with deliberate narrative function: the Aqedah tests whether Abraham will surrender the son of promise; immediately after, the narrative places before the reader the family from which the next generation of promise — Rebekah — will come. The introduction of Rebekah here (named for the first time at v. 23) is preparatory for Genesis 24. The genealogy is presented without comment, in standard *tōledôt* register, with the verbs *yāled* (יָלַד, "to father/beget") and *yāledāh* (יָלְדָה, "she fathered/bore"). Per project convention I render *yāled* as "fathered" for the masculine form (v. 23, Bethuel) and "bore" for the feminine (v. 23, Milcah; v. 24, Reumah).

**22:24 — "concubine" (*pîlagšô*, פִּילַגְשׁוֹ):**
The term *pîlegesh* (פִּילֶגֶשׁ) denotes a secondary wife or concubine — a legal category below the primary wife (*'iššāh*) but above a slave woman (*'āmāh*). I render it "concubine" per standard convention. This figure (Reumah) and her four sons expand Nahor's lineage beyond the eight children of Milcah (v. 23), totaling twelve descendants — a number that will echo in the twelve tribes of Israel and the twelve sons of Ishmael (Gen 25:12-16). The structural parallel is deliberate.

**LXX Vaticanus Observations (Pages 17–18):**
The Vaticanus text of Genesis 22 is clearly visible across pages 17–18. The scribe writes in a careful uncial hand without word breaks (scriptura continua). Page 17 carries the Aqedah narrative through approximately v. 18; page 18 continues with the genealogy (vv. 20–24) and proceeds into Genesis 23. A chapter marker *CAP. XXV* is visible at the top of page 18 (in the ancient Greek enumeration of chapters, not the modern one) and a marginal *CAP. IIII* notation appears at the right column. Most significant LXX variants for this chapter are noted in the individual verse annotations above. The most consequential divergences from the Masoretic text are: (1) at v. 2, LXX reads *agapētos* ("beloved") for *yāchîd* ("only"); (2) at v. 13, LXX reads *hena* ("one") for *'achar* ("behind"); (3) at v. 14, LXX renders the place name as "the Lord saw" (*Kyrios eiden*) and the proverb as "the Lord appeared/was seen" (*ōphthē*); (4) at v. 18, LXX uses unambiguous passive *eneulogēthēsontai* for the Hithpael *hitbarekhu*. These are not errors but interpretive translations that reflect distinct theological trajectories and became the basis for significant New Testament theology.

**DSS Note (4Q225 — Pseudo-Jubilees):**
Per the chapter manifest, Dead Sea Scroll fragment 4Q225 (Pseudo-Jubilees) contains a retelling of the Aqedah with interpretive expansions, including an explicit role for Mastema (a satanic adversary figure) as the instigator who challenges God to test Abraham — a motif paralleling the opening of Job. This expansion is not present in the canonical Hebrew text (Leningrad Codex) or the LXX; it represents a Second Temple period interpretive tradition that provides important context for understanding how the Aqedah was being read in the period before the New Testament. This DSS tradition is noted here for scholarly awareness but does not affect the translation of the canonical text.
